ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef yakiniku rice bowl; mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef based dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific yakiniku don code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with grilled beef slices, a half boiled egg, spring onion, yakiniku sauce, and Korean chilli pepper sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Half Boiled Egg Spring Onion Beef Yakiniku Bowl — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55 kcal/100g, protein 10.7 g/100g, fat 0.0 g/100g, carbohydrate 2.36 g/100g) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record for this dish (cr_id G00010196, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, saturated fat, sugar) estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite for beef yakiniku bowl components (USDA SR Legacy: cooked beef, white rice, egg, soy-based sauce) where HPB does not report them. fat_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is flagged as anomalous for a beef and egg dish and is preserved as reported with a caveat note. Confirmed zero per HPB source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice and mixed beef-rice dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef yakiniku bowl. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) diluted by the high protein content (10.7 g/100g) and low carbohydrate density (2.36 g/100g) of the dish as reported. The effective GI of the composite dish is substantially lower than plain rice due to protein-fat-fibre interaction. Analogous mixed rice-protein dish GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (range 45–60 for protein-enriched rice dishes) support this estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 8.26 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 4.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from yakiniku sauce (soy sauce base) and Korean chilli pepper sauce; estimated sodium ~320 mg/100g with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request yakiniku sauce on the side, limit sauce quantity, and choose a smaller rice portion to reduce overall sodium intake per meal, as the soy-sauce-based yakiniku and chilli sauces are the primary sodium contributors in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate density per 100g (2.36 g) with high protein (10.7 g) supports glycaemic stability, but the full bowl with steamed white rice substantially raises the glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total rice portion size carefully, request brown rice or a half-portion of rice, and ask for reduced yakiniku sauce to limit added sugars (mirin, sugar) that contribute to postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beef is a source of saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; yakiniku sauce contributes sodium; the egg yolk adds additional cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency to once or twice per week, request lean beef cuts, reduce sauce quantity to lower sodium and added sugar intake, and balance the meal with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA; dish lacks meaningful omega-3 or polyphenol anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with grilled chicken or salmon to reduce arachidonic acid load, request additional anti-inflammatory vegetables such as broccoli or spinach, and limit yakiniku sauce to reduce sodium-driven tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Spring Onion Beef Yakiniku Bowl (半熟卵ねぎ牛焼肉丼, Hanjuku Tamago Negi Gyū Yakiniku Don; Chinese: 半熟蛋葱牛烧肉饭) is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish served in Singapore, consisting of steamed white rice topped with thinly sliced grilled beef seasoned with yakiniku sauce (a sweet-savoury soy-mirin-based Japanese barbecue sauce), a soft half-boiled egg, sliced spring onion, and a sweet-spicy Korean chilli pepper sauce. The beef is typically pan-fried or grilled to a caramelised finish, yielding a tender, slightly charred texture with umami-rich, mildly sweet flavour. The half-boiled egg adds a creamy, runny yolk that enriches the sauce, while spring onion provides a fresh, pungent contrast. Per 100g as reported by HPB Singapore, the dish provides 55 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 0.0 g fat (anomalous — likely reflects a partial component record), and 2.36 g carbohydrate. The high protein density relative to caloric content makes this dish protein-efficient. Sodium is a clinical concern due to the soy-sauce-based yakiniku sauce and Korean chilli sauce. The dish is suitable for moderate consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes if rice portion is controlled, but is of higher concern for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis patients due to sodium load and red meat arachidonic acid content respectively. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore outlets due to the beef sourcing and sauce ingredients.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (confidence 0.95 per source record). fat_g reported as 0.0 by HPB for a beef-and-egg dish is anomalous and likely reflects a partial component record or data entry issue; this reduces overall confidence to medium. Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, dietary fibre, saturated fat) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite for beef yakiniku bowl components (USDA SR Legacy: cooked beef, white rice, egg, soy-based sauce) where HPB does not report them. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement.
notes: fat_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB Singapore source record (cr_id G00010196, confidence 0.95). This is anomalous for a dish containing beef and egg, and likely reflects that the HPB figures represent only the protein/sauce component of the dish rather than the full bowl including rice and egg yolk. fat_g is preserved as confirmed zero per HPB source record; however, the true fat content of the complete dish is expected to be higher. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB fat_g=0.0 report; confirmed zero per HPB source record. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Singapore Foodo for this record, and USDA SR Legacy fatty acid data cannot be reliably applied given the anomalous fat_g=0.0 base figure. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo for this dish record, and reliable estimation from USDA SR Legacy is not possible without confirmed fat and full macro basis. This dish is served in Singapore and carries a Japanese cuisine classification; accordingly, multilingual local names have been provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese to satisfy both the Singapore-served context and the Japanese cuisine G6 requirement.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per validator requirement.
Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per validator requirement.
Added Chinese simplified (半熟蛋葱牛烧肉饭), Chinese traditional (半熟蛋蔥牛燒肉飯), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to local_names to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore-served dish context.
Added all new local-language names to aliases_by_language (chinese, malay, tamil, hindi arrays) and to the flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 and G6.
Verified aliases consistency: every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in flat aliases array.
